title: The mediatization of urban development and Egypt's New Administrative Capital
data_sources: Ministry of Housing 'The Capital Egypt' YouTube channel

data_scope.outlet: Al-Ahram, Egypt's most-circulated state-regulated daily (daily circ. ~1.2M, weekly ~1.4M)
data_scope.case_site: New Administrative Capital, ~60 km east of Cairo, ~170,000 feddan (~714 km2), stated ~US$45B investment
data_scope.geospatial_datasets: none (qualitative text study; no GIS, census, or remote-sensing data)

core_conclusion: National press coverage of Egypt's New Administrative Capital did not merely report the megaproject but helped construct it. Across 111 Al-Ahram headlines (2015-2019), framing shifted from investment opportunity and solution to Cairo's urban problems (2015-2017) toward spectacular physical landmarks (2017-2019), privileging visual enticement and short-term financial returns while affordability and accessibility for the urban majority received little attention. Media and planning mutually reinforce one another, enabling exclusive, neoliberal urban growth through virtual, visual and discursive means.
why_it_matters: Operationalizes mediatization to show media as an active force in city-making; documents a measurable narrative drift coinciding with the 2017 launch of the first gated compound (Midtown); surfaces an affordability/accessibility blind spot in public discourse; offers a framework transferable to new-capital and megaproject contexts worldwide.
why_nature: Treats the city as a coupled human-media-space system; reads a Global South megaproject as a generalizable neoliberal pattern; bridges urban planning, design, media studies and policy around equity and the public interest; offers policy-relevant critique of how public media blur public/private boundaries.
added_value_to_my_research: Scale 111 hand-coded headlines into a reproducible multilingual NLP discourse + sentiment pipeline (FAIR/open-science)
Geolocate the discourse and compute 2SFCA/R2SFCA access for relocated and neighboring-city residents to test the affordability gap
Run a large-scale visual-perception study on NAC renderings to operationalize 'visual enticement' and probe demographic bias
Build geotagged social-media sentiment series to contrast bottom-up affordability anxieties with top-down spectacle framing
Design and evaluate public-interest counter-narratives for healthy-cities and equitable-access policy
